I Spent Ten Years Getting Faster at Writing Code. It Was the Wrong Thing to Get Good At, and I Found Out the Hard Way.

I optimized relentlessly for output. Type faster, ship faster, close more tickets. I was proud of it. And then the ground shifted under the exact skill I had spent a decade sharpening, and I had to ask a question I had been avoiding: was speed ever the thing that made me valuable, or just the thing I could measure?

6 min read
Just now

--

For most of my career I optimized for the wrong variable, and I did it with discipline, and I was proud of it the whole time, which is the part that stings.
The variable was speed. Output. How fast I could turn a specification into working code. I got good at it, genuinely good. I knew my tools cold, I could move through a codebase quickly, I closed tickets faster than most people around me, and I wore that as a professional identity. I was the guy who shipped. Give me a well-defined task and I would have it done before you finished explaining the next one.
For years, that was rewarded. Velocity was visible, velocity was praised, velocity got me raises and a reputation. So I doubled down on it, the way you double down on anything that gets rewarded, and I sharpened the one skill, the speed, into the finest edge I could, and I did not think very hard about whether it was the right skill, because it was working and questioning it felt unnecessary.
Then the ground moved, and I had to look directly at a question I had spent a decade not asking.
The question I had been avoiding
The question is this: was the speed ever actually the thing that made me valuable, or was it just the thing I could see and measure and therefore optimize?
Because here is what happened. The tools got good at the fast part. Really good. The turning-a-spec-into-code part, the thing I had built my identity on, the thing I was proud of being fast at, became something a machine could do faster than me and without getting tired. And I felt the floor tilt, because if my value was speed, and speed just got automated, then what exactly was I.
I sat with that, uncomfortably, for a while. And the honest answer, when I finally let myself look at it, was worse than I wanted and also, eventually, freeing. The speed was never the thing. The speed was the thing I could measure, so I had mistaken it for the thing that mattered, the way you look for your keys under the streetlight because that is where the light is, not because that is where you dropped them.
What I had actually been doing all along
When I looked honestly at the times I had been most valuable in my career, they were almost never the times I was fast.
They were the times I stopped someone from building the wrong thing. The times I looked at a plan everyone was excited about and said, quietly, “wait, what happens in this case,” and saved months. The times I was the only one in the incident who stayed calm and asked the right question. The times I said “we do not need this complexity” and was right. The times I understood, from years of scar tissue, why a thing that looked fine was actually dangerous.
None of that was speed. All of it was judgment. And I had been treating those moments as incidental, as the stuff around the real work, when they were the real work and the fast typing was the incidental part. I had my whole career backwards. I thought I was a fast implementer who occasionally had good judgment. I was actually a decent judgment-haver who had been hiding it, even from myself, behind a fast-typing identity that was easier to measure and prouder to claim.
The tools automating my speed did not take my value. They took my disguise. They removed the thing I had been pointing at when someone asked what I did, and forced me to notice that the thing I had actually been doing, the whole time, was standing quietly behind it, unmeasured and unnamed and much more important.
Why I had built it wrong
I think I know why I optimized for speed, and I think it is common, and I think it is worth naming because a lot of engineers are about to have the reckoning I had.
Speed is legible. It is countable. Tickets closed, features shipped, lines written, velocity charted. You can see it, your manager can see it, it goes in a performance review, it feels like progress because it has a number attached. Judgment has no number. You cannot count the disasters you prevented, because prevented disasters do not happen and unhappened things do not show up on a dashboard. The value of the architecture call you got right is invisible precisely because everything worked, so nothing dramatic occurred, so there is nothing to point at.
So I optimized the thing with the number and neglected the thing without one, not because I was foolish, but because the thing with the number kept telling me I was winning, and the thing without one stayed silent even when it was the entire reason I was worth keeping. I built my career on the measurable half of my job and let the unmeasurable, more valuable half take care of itself, and I got away with it right up until the measurable half got automated and I had to go find out whether the other half was real.
It was real. That is the good news, and it is the whole point. But I did not know it was real until I was forced to look, and a lot of engineers are going to be forced to look, and some of them are going to discover their judgment was real too, and some are going to discover they spent ten years so focused on speed that they never developed the other thing, and those are going to be two very different next decades.
What I would tell my younger self
Stop optimizing for the thing you can measure just because you can measure it. The speed was never going to be your moat. It was always going to be automatable, because anything that clean and countable and repeatable was always going to be the first thing a machine learned to do.
The thing that lasts is the judgment, the deciding, the knowing-why, the calm in the incident, the “wait, what about this case,” the refusal to build the complex thing when the simple thing would do. It is unmeasurable, which is exactly why it is durable, because unmeasurable things are hard to automate and hard to outsource and hard to replace. I spent a decade sharpening the wrong edge because it was the one I could see myself sharpening. Do not do that. Develop the quiet skill that does not show up on any chart, because that is the one that will still be yours when the loud, measurable one belongs to the machines.
I got lucky. The judgment was there, underneath, when I finally went looking, built up almost by accident over years of paying attention. But it was luck, and I would rather you build it on purpose than find out by accident whether you happened to develop it while you were busy being fast.
The typing is being automated. It was always going to be. The question I wish I had asked ten years earlier is the one worth asking now: what are you good at that cannot be counted, and are you developing it, or are you just getting faster at the thing that was never going to be enough.
Ten years of the judgment layer, the deciding and the knowing-why that does not automate, is what I tried to compress into The Senior Backend Engineer Handbook so you can build the durable skill on purpose instead of hoping you picked it up by accident. That is the whole pitch. The rest of what I think goes out through my newsletter.
